questions.text: A standard soccer match lasts for 90 minutes.
questions.explanation: Two halves of 45 minutes.

questions.text: In tennis, the term 'love' means a score of zero.
questions.explanation: It's believed to come from the French word 'l'oeuf' meaning egg (shaped like a zero).

questions.text: Michael Jordan played professional baseball.
questions.explanation: He played Minor League Baseball for the Chicago White Sox organization.

questions.text: The Olympics are held every three years.
questions.explanation: Summer and Winter Olympics are each held every four years.

questions.text: Tug of war is still contested at the modern Summer Olympics today.
questions.explanation: It was an Olympic sport from 1900 to 1920 but was dropped and is no longer contested.

questions.text: The earliest golf balls were solid carved wood with no covering.
questions.explanation: Early balls were 'featheries' — leather pouches stuffed with feathers, not solid wood.

questions.text: A bowling pin must fully topple flat to count as knocked down.
questions.explanation: A pin only needs to fall, not lie flat; pins tip with little more than a 7.5 degree lean.

questions.text: Babe Ruth holds the record for the most home runs in MLB history.
questions.explanation: Barry Bonds holds the record, followed by Hank Aaron.

questions.text: No boxer in history has ever retired with an undefeated record.
questions.explanation: Rocky Marciano and Floyd Mayweather Jr. both retired undefeated.

questions.text: The longest recorded tennis match lasted over 11 hours.
questions.explanation: John Isner and Nicolas Mahut played for 11 hours and 5 minutes at Wimbledon in 2010.

questions.text: Basketball was originally played with wooden buckets.
questions.explanation: James Naismith used peach baskets, not wooden buckets.

questions.text: The Tour de France was created to sell newspapers.
questions.explanation: It was launched as a publicity stunt by the sports newspaper L Auto in 1903.

questions.text: The Olympic torch has never accidentally gone out.
questions.explanation: It has been extinguished several times during relay events and discreetly relit.

questions.text: Table tennis began as an outdoor lawn sport before moving indoors.
questions.explanation: It started as an indoor after-dinner parlour game in Victorian England.

questions.text: Muhammad Ali was born with the name Michael Williams.
questions.explanation: He was born Cassius Marcellus Clay Jr. and changed his name in 1964.

questions.text: Wimbledon requires all players to wear all-white clothing.
questions.explanation: The All England Club strictly enforces this tradition to this day.

questions.text: Hockey pucks are frozen before NHL games.
questions.explanation: Frozen pucks slide faster and bounce less unpredictably on the ice.

questions.text: The longest professional boxing match on record went exactly 15 rounds.
questions.explanation: An 1893 bout lasted 110 rounds and over seven hours before being called.

questions.text: An NFL game contains only about 11 minutes of actual playing time.
questions.explanation: The rest is replays, huddles, clock stoppages, and commercials.

questions.text: Michael Phelps won fewer gold medals than most Olympic countries.
questions.explanation: His 23 gold medals exceed the all-time totals of the majority of participating nations.

questions.text: Golf was once made compulsory for all men in medieval Scotland.
questions.explanation: It was banned in 1457 because men were skipping archery practice to play.

questions.text: Volleyball was invented as a tougher, faster alternative to basketball.
questions.explanation: It was created in 1895 as a gentler game for older businessmen.

questions.text: Players have used the Stanley Cup as a dog bowl.
questions.explanation: Players get personal days with the cup and many have let pets eat from it.

questions.text: Baseball umpires are required to wear bright red undershirts by rule.
questions.explanation: The tradition is dark underwear, so nothing bright shows if their pants split.

questions.text: Sumo wrestlers must weigh over 150 kg to compete professionally.
questions.explanation: There is no minimum weight rule in sumo; technique is what matters.

questions.text: Early tennis was first played using wooden paddles, never the bare hand.
questions.explanation: Early 'jeu de paume' was literally played with the open palm of the hand.

questions.text: Olympic gold medals are made of solid gold.
questions.explanation: Modern gold medals are mostly silver with only a gold plating.

questions.text: Usain Bolt top recorded speed was over 35 mph.
questions.explanation: His top recorded speed was 27.78 mph, still extraordinarily fast.

questions.text: Competitive eating is an official Olympic sport.
questions.explanation: It has never been part of the Olympic program.

questions.text: The first modern Olympic Games were held in Rome in 1900.
questions.explanation: The first modern Olympics were held in Athens in 1896.

questions.text: In the original Olympic Games, athletes competed in the nude.
questions.explanation: Greek athletes competed naked as a celebration of the human form.

questions.text: A cricket ball is heavier than a tennis ball.
questions.explanation: Cricket balls weigh 155-163g; tennis balls are only 56-59g.

questions.text: Cristiano Ronaldo has scored more career goals than any other footballer in history.
questions.explanation: He holds the record for most career goals in recognized competitive football.

questions.text: The longest recorded tennis rally lasted fewer than 100 shots.
questions.explanation: A recorded practice rally reached 643 shots.

questions.text: Chess has a world governing body recognized by the International Olympic Committee.
questions.explanation: FIDE, chess's governing body, is recognized by the IOC, though chess isn't an Olympic event.

questions.text: Serena Williams won a Grand Slam title while pregnant.
questions.explanation: She won the 2017 Australian Open in the early weeks of her pregnancy.

questions.text: The New York Yankees have the most World Series titles.
questions.explanation: They have won 27 World Series championships.

questions.text: In soccer, a hat trick means scoring three goals in one match.
questions.explanation: Three goals by one player in a single game is a hat trick.

questions.text: The fastest recorded tennis serve exceeded 250 km per hour.
questions.explanation: Sam Groth holds the record at 263.4 km per hour.

questions.text: NBA hoops are exactly 12 feet off the ground.
questions.explanation: They are exactly 10 feet (3.05 meters) — unchanged since 1891.

questions.text: Polo was played in ancient Persia more than a thousand years ago.
questions.explanation: Polo has roots in ancient Persia, where it was played by cavalry centuries ago.

questions.text: Tiger Woods won his first major championship before turning professional.
questions.explanation: He turned pro in 1996 and won his first major, the Masters, in 1997 as a pro.

questions.text: The Super Bowl is the most-watched annual television broadcast in the United States.
questions.explanation: It routinely draws the largest US TV audience of the year.

questions.text: An ice hockey puck travels faster than 160 km per hour during play.
questions.explanation: The hardest slap shots have been recorded above 190 km per hour.

questions.text: Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup more times than any other nation.
questions.explanation: Brazil holds the record with five World Cup titles.

questions.text: In baseball, three strikes means you are out.
questions.explanation: A fundamental rule of the sport since its codification in 1845.

questions.text: Jockeys in horse racing must weigh under 60 kg.
questions.explanation: Weight limits vary by race, but many races allow up to around 63 kg including equipment.

questions.text: The Olympic rings represent the five continents of the world.
questions.explanation: Africa, Americas, Asia, Europe, and Oceania, united by the interlinked rings.

questions.text: Badminton shuttlecocks can travel faster than 300 km per hour.
questions.explanation: The world record smash speed is 493 km per hour, making it the fastest projectile in sport.

questions.text: Golf is the only sport that has been played on the Moon.
questions.explanation: Alan Shepard hit two golf balls during Apollo 14 in 1971.

questions.text: A boxing ring is shaped like a perfect circle.
questions.explanation: It's square - the name 'ring' survived from older roped-circle fights.

questions.text: Table tennis was once banned in the Soviet Union for harming eyesight.
questions.explanation: The USSR banned it in the 1930s-40s believing it damaged players' eyes.

questions.text: The Olympic marathon distance is exactly 42 kilometers.
questions.explanation: It's 42.195 km, set at the 1908 London Games to reach the royal box.

questions.text: Cricket Test matches can last up to five days and still end in a draw.
questions.explanation: Five days of play can finish with no winner if no result is reached.

questions.text: The first ball used in basketball was a soccer ball.
questions.explanation: Naismith used a soccer ball in 1891 before purpose-made balls existed.

questions.text: Formula 1 tyres are filled with regular compressed air.
questions.explanation: They use nitrogen, which keeps pressure stable as temperatures swing.

questions.text: An NFL football game has 60 minutes of actual game-clock time.
questions.explanation: Four 15-minute quarters, though real time runs over three hours.

questions.text: Wimbledon tennis balls are stored at room temperature before matches.
questions.explanation: They're kept at 20C in special containers for consistent bounce.

questions.text: A regulation baseball has exactly 108 double stitches.
questions.explanation: Every MLB ball is hand-stitched with 108 red double stitches.

questions.text: The Stanley Cup has had names misspelled and engraved with errors.
questions.explanation: Multiple typos, like 'Bostq Bruins', remain etched on the trophy.

questions.text: Chess boxing alternates rounds of chess and boxing in a single match.
questions.explanation: A real hybrid sport where you can win by checkmate or knockout.

questions.text: The Tour de France has always been raced entirely within France.
questions.explanation: It regularly crosses into neighbours and has even started abroad.

questions.text: Pelota's ball can travel faster than 300 kilometers per hour in jai alai.
questions.explanation: Jai alai is among the fastest ball sports, clocking past 300 km/h.

questions.text: An NBA player once scored 100 points in a single game.
questions.explanation: Wilt Chamberlain dropped 100 for the Warriors in 1962.

questions.text: The original Olympic torch relay tradition dates back to ancient Greece.
questions.explanation: The modern relay was invented for the 1936 Berlin Games.

questions.text: Croquet appeared as an Olympic sport with paying spectators in attendance.
questions.explanation: The 1900 Paris croquet event reportedly drew just one paying spectator.

questions.text: Cricket's longest Test match lasted ten days and still ended without a winner.
questions.explanation: The 1939 'Timeless Test' in Durban was abandoned so England could catch their ship home.

questions.text: A perfect 10 has been the maximum score in Olympic gymnastics since 2006.
questions.explanation: The 10.0 cap was scrapped in 2006 for an open-ended difficulty scoring system.

questions.text: A football goalkeeper has scored a goal directly from inside his own half.
questions.explanation: Keepers like Tom King have scored from clearances over 90 metres.

questions.text: The Indianapolis 500 is named for the number of laps drivers complete.
questions.explanation: It's named for the 500-mile distance: 200 laps of the 2.5-mile oval.

questions.text: Synchronized swimming allowed male competitors at the Olympics before 2024.
questions.explanation: Men were first permitted in the Olympic event only at Paris 2024.

questions.text: The world record for farthest accurate archery shot was set by an archer with no arms.
questions.explanation: Matt Stutzman hit a target at 283.47m in 2015, shooting with his feet.

questions.text: Wilt Chamberlain's single-game rebound record has since been broken.
questions.explanation: His 55-rebound game from 1960 still stands as the NBA record.

questions.text: The first modern Olympic marathon in 1896 was won by a Greek water carrier.
questions.explanation: Spyridon Louis, a water carrier, won to national jubilation in Athens.

questions.text: Polo at the Olympics was once contested using motorcycles instead of horses.
questions.explanation: Olympic polo was always on horseback; motoball was never an Olympic event.

questions.text: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain above sea level.
questions.explanation: It stands at 8,848 meters.

questions.text: The Great Wall of China is visible from space with the naked eye.
questions.explanation: It is a myth; you cannot see it without aid from low Earth orbit.

questions.text: There are 50 states in the United States.
questions.explanation: There are exactly 50 states.

questions.text: Napoleon Bonaparte was extremely short.
questions.explanation: He was around 5'6", which was average height for a Frenchman at the time.

questions.text: Oxford University is older than the Aztec Empire.
questions.explanation: Oxford was founded around 1096, while the city of Tenochtitlán was founded in 1325.

questions.text: Vikings wore horned helmets in battle.
questions.explanation: There is no historical evidence that Vikings ever wore horned helmets.

questions.text: Toasters were invented before pre-sliced bread.
questions.explanation: The electric toaster was invented in 1893, while sliced bread came out in 1928.

questions.text: Humans use only 10% of their brains.
questions.explanation: We use virtually every part of the brain, and most parts are active almost all the time.

questions.text: Florida is the closest US state to the continent of Africa.
questions.explanation: Maine's eastern tip is actually closer to Africa than any part of Florida.

questions.text: Cleopatra lived closer to the iPhone's invention than to the building of the Great Pyramid.
questions.explanation: She lived around 30 BC, whereas the Great Pyramid was built around 2560 BC.

questions.text: Fortune cookies were invented in China.
questions.explanation: They were invented in California in the early 1900s.

questions.text: Brazil has more lakes than any other country.
questions.explanation: Canada has approximately 2 million lakes — more than the rest of the world combined.

questions.text: There is no McDonald's restaurant anywhere in Antarctica.
questions.explanation: Antarctica has no permanent commercial fast-food outlets.

questions.text: Australia is wider than the Moon.
questions.explanation: Australia spans almost 4000 km across; the Moon is only 3474 km in diameter.

questions.text: English is the official national language of the United States.
questions.explanation: The US has no federally designated official language.

questions.text: French fries were invented in France.
questions.explanation: They are believed to have originated in Belgium.

questions.text: The letter Q does not appear in any US state name.
questions.explanation: Check all 50 state names and you will find not a single Q.

questions.text: Hawaii is both the westernmost and easternmost US state.
questions.explanation: Alaska holds that distinction; its Aleutians cross the 180-degree meridian.

questions.text: Pineapples grow on trees and ripen within a few weeks.
questions.explanation: Pineapples grow on ground-level plants and take about two years to mature.

questions.text: The national animal of Scotland is the golden eagle.
questions.explanation: Scotland's national animal is the mythical unicorn.

questions.text: Humans can fully digest cellulose from plants.
questions.explanation: We lack the cellulase enzyme, so dietary fiber passes through us undigested.

questions.text: Tsar Nicholas II and King George V of England were unrelated lookalikes.
questions.explanation: They were first cousins, which is why they looked nearly identical.

questions.text: Strawberries have their seeds on the inside.
questions.explanation: Strawberries are the only fruit with seeds on the outside — each dot is a tiny achene.

questions.text: Mount Chimborazo is closer to outer space than Mount Everest.
questions.explanation: The equatorial bulge means Chimborazo peak is the farthest point from Earth center.

questions.text: Honey never expires.
questions.explanation: Honey found in Egyptian tombs over 3000 years old was still perfectly edible.

questions.text: The hashtag symbol is formally known as an 'asterisk-grid'.
questions.explanation: Its formal name is 'octothorpe', coined at Bell Labs in the 1960s.

questions.text: A baker's dozen is 12 because bakers wanted to save money.
questions.explanation: A baker's dozen is actually 13 — medieval bakers added an extra to avoid fines.

questions.text: Nintendo was founded in 1889.
questions.explanation: It started as a playing card company, long before video games existed.

questions.text: The inventor of the World Wide Web sold the patent for a fortune.
questions.explanation: Tim Berners-Lee gave the web away for free; he was later knighted.

questions.text: Oxford and Cambridge universities have their own railway stations.
questions.explanation: Both cities have their own mainline rail connections.

questions.text: Iceland has no army.
questions.explanation: Iceland has a Coast Guard and a Crisis Response Unit but no traditional standing army.

questions.text: The Sahara Desert is the largest hot desert in the world.
questions.explanation: It covers about 9.2 million square kilometres across North Africa.

questions.text: IKEA is named after the Swedish word for 'home furnishing'.
questions.explanation: It's an acronym from founder Ingvar Kamprad's name, farm, and village.

questions.text: No piece of paper can be folded in half more than eight times.
questions.explanation: Britney Gallivan folded a piece of toilet paper 12 times in 2002, disproving this myth.

questions.text: The phrase 'Elementary, my dear Watson' never appears in Conan Doyle's original Sherlock Holmes stories.
questions.explanation: The exact phrase was popularized by later films, not the books.

questions.text: Cashews grow inside a fruit.
questions.explanation: The cashew apple is a swollen stem; the nut hangs below it in a shell.

questions.text: The pound sterling sign comes from a stylized letter P for 'pound'.
questions.explanation: It comes from 'libra', the Latin word for scales, hence the L shape.

questions.text: The average person walks the equivalent of once around Earth in a lifetime.
questions.explanation: We actually walk about five times around the Earth over a lifetime.

questions.text: Bubble wrap was first invented as packaging for electronics.
questions.explanation: Its inventors first tried to market it as textured wallpaper in 1957.

questions.text: The shortest commercial flight in the world lasts 90 seconds.
questions.explanation: The Westray to Papa Westray flight in Scotland can take under two minutes.

questions.text: Japan is the most visited country in the world.
questions.explanation: France consistently tops global tourism rankings with about 90 million visitors annually.

questions.text: A group of pandas is called an embarrassment.
questions.explanation: One of the more entertaining official collective nouns.

questions.text: The Pacific Ocean is saltier than the Atlantic Ocean.
questions.explanation: The Atlantic is saltier, due to more evaporation and less rainfall.

questions.text: A week has seven days.
questions.explanation: The seven-day week traces back to ancient Babylonian astronomy and is now near-universal.

questions.text: Goldfish have a memory span of only three seconds.
questions.explanation: They can remember things for months; the three-second myth has been repeatedly debunked.

questions.text: Bananas grow on trees.
questions.explanation: The banana plant is a giant herb; its 'trunk' is made of tightly packed leaf bases.

questions.text: The Eiffel Tower can grow more than 15 centimeters taller in summer heat.
questions.explanation: Iron expands when heated, so the tower physically grows on hot days.

questions.text: There are countries with no rivers at all.
questions.explanation: Saudi Arabia, Qatar, and several others have no permanent natural rivers.

questions.text: The shortest war in recorded history lasted under an hour.
questions.explanation: The 1896 Anglo-Zanzibar War ended in roughly 38 minutes.

questions.text: Vatican City is the smallest country in the world by area.
questions.explanation: At about 0.49 square kilometers, it's smaller than many city parks.

questions.text: The plural of octopus is officially octopi.
questions.explanation: The word is Greek, not Latin, so 'octopuses' or 'octopodes' is more correct.

questions.text: Russia spans eleven time zones.
questions.explanation: It's the widest country on Earth, stretching across eleven official time zones.

questions.text: The dot over a lowercase i or j has its own name.
questions.explanation: It's called a 'tittle', from the same Latin root as 'title'.

questions.text: The Canary Islands were named after canary birds.
questions.explanation: They were named for dogs ('canis' in Latin); the bird was named after the islands.

questions.text: Finland has more reindeer than people.
questions.explanation: Finland is famous for having more saunas than cars, not reindeer than people.

questions.text: The longest place name in the world has over 80 letters.
questions.explanation: A New Zealand hill named Taumata...whenuakitanatahu runs to 85 letters.

questions.text: Antarctica is the only continent with no native species of spider.
questions.explanation: It's the only continent with no native ant species; spiders aren't the fact.

questions.text: The word 'set' has historically had the longest entry of any word in the Oxford English Dictionary.
questions.explanation: Older OED editions gave 'set' more distinct senses than any other word.

questions.text: The Pacific Ocean is wider than the Moon.
questions.explanation: At about 19,000 km across, the Pacific dwarfs the Moon's 3,475 km diameter.

questions.text: The spiciness of chili peppers is measured in officially named Scoville Heat Units.
questions.explanation: Pharmacist Wilbur Scoville devised the scale in 1912 to rate pepper heat.

questions.text: The metric system was first officially adopted by the United States in the 1970s.
questions.explanation: The US still uses customary units; it has never fully adopted the metric system.

questions.text: Saudi Arabia exports construction sand to the rest of the world.
questions.explanation: It imports sand, because its smooth desert sand is poor for construction.

questions.text: The 'black box' flight recorder on aircraft is painted bright orange.
questions.explanation: It's orange for visibility in wreckage; 'black box' is just a nickname.

questions.text: There is enough water in Lake Baikal to supply the entire world for decades.
questions.explanation: Baikal holds about a fifth of all unfrozen freshwater on Earth's surface.

questions.text: Bhutan was among the last countries in the world to introduce television, doing so in 1999.
questions.explanation: Bhutan lifted its TV ban only in 1999, one of the very last nations to allow it.

questions.text: The sun rises in the East and sets in the West.
questions.explanation: Due to Earth's rotation, this is true.

questions.text: Water makes up about 71% of the Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Oceans hold about 96.5% of all Earth's water.

questions.text: Lightning never strikes the same place twice.
questions.explanation: Lightning constantly strikes tall structures like the Empire State Building.

questions.text: Venus is the hottest planet in our solar system.
questions.explanation: Even though Mercury is closer, Venus has a thick atmosphere that traps heat.

questions.text: Sound travels faster through water than through air.
questions.explanation: Water's tightly packed molecules carry sound vibrations faster than air does.

questions.text: Bananas are strictly considered berries, but strawberries are not.
questions.explanation: Botanically, bananas are berries, while strawberries are 'aggregate fruits'.

questions.text: Around one thousand Earths could fit inside the Sun.
questions.explanation: About 1.3 million Earths could fit inside the Sun, not a thousand.

questions.text: A year on Venus is longer than a day on Venus.
questions.explanation: It's the reverse: a Venus day is longer than its year because it rotates so slowly.

questions.text: Glass is a slow-moving liquid, which is why old windows are thicker at the bottom.
questions.explanation: Glass is an amorphous solid; old panes are uneven due to early manufacturing methods.

questions.text: There are more trees on Earth than stars in the Milky Way.
questions.explanation: There are ~3 trillion trees on Earth, compared to ~100-400 billion stars in the Milky Way.

questions.text: Carrots dramatically improve your night vision.
questions.explanation: You would need a severe deficiency to see any real benefit from extra carrots.

questions.text: Warm water generally comes to a boil faster than cold water.
questions.explanation: Warmer water starts closer to boiling, so it needs less energy to get there.

questions.text: Diamonds are made from compressed coal.
questions.explanation: They form from pure carbon under extreme pressure, before coal-producing plants even existed.

questions.text: Helium is the only element that cannot solidify at normal atmospheric pressure.
questions.explanation: Quantum effects prevent it from solidifying without added pressure.

questions.text: The universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old.
questions.explanation: Based on measurements of cosmic background radiation.

questions.text: Peanuts are not true nuts but are legumes.
questions.explanation: They grow in pods underground, related to beans and lentils.

questions.text: An atom is mostly made of densely packed solid matter.
questions.explanation: An atom is almost entirely empty space; the nucleus is tiny relative to the whole.

questions.text: Silver is a better electrical conductor than gold.
questions.explanation: Silver is the best conductor; gold is used mainly for corrosion resistance.

questions.text: Tomatoes are botanically classified as fruit.
questions.explanation: Any structure enclosing seeds that develops from a flower is botanically a fruit.

questions.text: All neon signs use neon gas.
questions.explanation: Only reddish-orange signs use pure neon; other colors use argon, krypton, or other gases.

questions.text: The Sun appears yellow from Earth but is truly a white star.
questions.explanation: Earth's atmosphere scatters blue light, giving the Sun a yellowish tint from the ground.

questions.text: Rubber is an effective electrical insulator.
questions.explanation: Its resistance to current is why wires are coated in it.

questions.text: The human eye can distinguish only a few thousand distinct colors.
questions.explanation: The eye can distinguish roughly ten million colors.

questions.text: Wi-Fi stands for Wireless Fidelity.
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi is a trademarked name with no official meaning; it was coined as a play on Hi-Fi.

questions.text: The human stomach lining is the same one you are born with for life.
questions.explanation: The stomach lining is replaced every few days because acid is so corrosive.

questions.text: Humans and T-Rex are closer in time than T-Rex and Stegosaurus.
questions.explanation: Stegosaurus lived 80 million years before T-Rex; we are only 65 million years from T-Rex.

questions.text: There are more possible chess games than atoms in the observable universe.
questions.explanation: The Shannon number estimates 10 to the power of 120 possible chess games.

questions.text: Silicon is the most abundant element in the Earth's crust.
questions.explanation: Oxygen is most abundant, making up about 46 percent of the crust by mass.

questions.text: Lightning is hotter than the surface of the sun.
questions.explanation: A lightning bolt reaches 30,000 Kelvin; the sun surface is about 5,778 Kelvin.

questions.text: Humans can smell rain because of a chemical called petrichor.
questions.explanation: Petrichor is produced when rain hits dry earth, releasing oils and geosmin.

questions.text: Fingernails and toenails grow at exactly the same rate.
questions.explanation: Fingernails grow faster than toenails, and faster on the dominant hand.

questions.text: The appendix has no function in the human body.
questions.explanation: It is believed to serve as a reservoir for gut bacteria and may play an immune role.

questions.text: Adults have more bones than babies.
questions.explanation: Babies have about 300 bones; many fuse as we grow, leaving adults with just 206.

questions.text: Dark matter makes up the majority of the universe.
questions.explanation: About 27 percent dark matter, 68 percent dark energy, and only 5 percent normal matter.

questions.text: Freshly cut grass smell is simply the scent of chlorophyll.
questions.explanation: That smell is a chemical distress signal released by the damaged grass.

questions.text: Stainless steel door handles naturally kill germs on contact.
questions.explanation: Copper, not steel, has antimicrobial properties that kill microbes over hours.

questions.text: You can physically see the Milky Way galaxy from Earth.
questions.explanation: We are inside it, so we see it as a band of stars across the night sky.

questions.text: There are more grains of sand on Earth than stars in the universe.
questions.explanation: Estimates suggest the reverse: more stars in the universe than sand grains on Earth.

questions.text: Clouds are weightless.
questions.explanation: An average cumulus cloud weighs about 500 tonnes.

questions.text: The human brain is about three-quarters water.
questions.explanation: Roughly 75 percent of the brain's mass is water.

questions.text: Two clean pieces of metal will never bond just by touching in space.
questions.explanation: They can cold-weld in vacuum because there's no oxide layer to stop bonding.

questions.text: Gravity travels at the speed of light.
questions.explanation: Gravitational waves were confirmed to travel at exactly the speed of light.

questions.text: Humans have five senses and no more.
questions.explanation: We have additional senses like balance and proprioception, the sense of body position.

questions.text: Light travels faster than sound.
questions.explanation: Light outpaces sound by roughly a million times, which is why you see lightning before thunder.

questions.text: Saturn is light enough that it would float in a large enough body of water.
questions.explanation: Its average density is about 0.69 g/cm3, less than water's, thanks to its gassy makeup.

questions.text: The human tongue has specific zones that detect each taste.
questions.explanation: The 'tongue map' is a myth; all taste types are detected across the whole tongue.

questions.text: Hot water can freeze faster than cold water under the right conditions.
questions.explanation: Known as the Mpemba effect, it's been observed though its causes are still debated.

questions.text: Your body contains roughly as many bacterial cells as human cells.
questions.explanation: The old 10-to-1 claim was debunked; the real ratio is closer to 1.3 to 1.

questions.text: Sharks existed before trees did.
questions.explanation: Sharks date back over 400 million years, predating the first trees by tens of millions.

questions.text: Stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve some metals.
questions.explanation: At a pH near 1.5, stomach acid can corrode materials like zinc.

questions.text: A bolt of lightning contains enough energy to toast thousands of slices of bread.
questions.explanation: A single strike packs roughly a billion joules of energy.

questions.text: The Sahara is the largest desert on Earth.
questions.explanation: Antarctica is the largest desert; a desert is defined by low precipitation, not heat.

questions.text: Hair and fingernails keep growing for a while after a person dies.
questions.explanation: They don't grow; skin dehydrates and retracts, making them only appear longer.

questions.text: There is no gravity in outer space.
questions.explanation: Astronauts float because they're in continuous free fall, not because gravity is absent.

questions.text: Octopuses have three hearts and blue blood.
questions.explanation: Two hearts pump to the gills, one to the body; copper-based hemocyanin makes blood blue.

questions.text: A teaspoon of neutron star material would weigh about the same as a car.
questions.explanation: It would weigh billions of tonnes; neutron star matter is extraordinarily dense.

questions.text: The Great Pyramid contains enough stone to be seen glowing from heat at night.
questions.explanation: Stone doesn't glow from stored heat; this is fabricated pseudo-science.

questions.text: Helium can make your voice higher and a heavier gas can make it deeper.
questions.explanation: Sound moves faster in helium and slower in dense gases like sulfur hexafluoride.

questions.text: Light from the Sun takes about eight minutes to reach Earth.
questions.explanation: At 150 million km away, sunlight takes roughly 8 minutes and 20 seconds to arrive.

questions.text: Water can exist as solid, liquid, and gas at the same time under specific conditions.
questions.explanation: At water's 'triple point' of temperature and pressure, all three states coexist.

questions.text: DNA from two unrelated humans is on average about 90 percent identical.
questions.explanation: Any two humans share about 99.9 percent of their DNA, not 90 percent.

questions.text: It rains diamonds on Neptune and Uranus.
questions.explanation: Extreme pressure squeezes carbon in their atmospheres into diamond, which sinks as rain.

questions.text: Time passes very slightly faster for your head than for your feet when standing.
questions.explanation: General relativity says gravity slows time, so time runs faster farther from Earth's center.

questions.text: More than 99 percent of the atoms in your body were forged inside stars.
questions.explanation: Elements heavier than hydrogen and helium were made in stars, then scattered by supernovae.

questions.text: Voyager 1 is the first human-made object to reach interstellar space.
questions.explanation: Launched in 1977, it crossed beyond the Sun's bubble into interstellar space in 2012.

questions.text: Bees are unable to tell individual human faces apart.
questions.explanation: Studies show honeybees can learn to recognize individual human faces.

questions.text: Absolute zero, the coldest possible temperature, has been reached in a lab.
questions.explanation: Scientists get extremely close but absolute zero is physically unreachable.

questions.text: A photon of light can take tens of thousands of years to travel from the Sun's core to its surface.
questions.explanation: Energy zigzags through dense plasma for millennia before escaping as sunlight.

questions.text: Bananas are completely free of any natural radioactivity.
questions.explanation: Bananas contain potassium-40, making them slightly and harmlessly radioactive.

questions.text: Sound can travel through the vacuum of space if the source is loud enough.
questions.explanation: Sound needs a medium; a vacuum has no particles to carry the vibrations.

questions.text: Tardigrades can survive the vacuum and radiation of open space.
questions.explanation: These tiny 'water bears' survived direct exposure to space in a 2007 experiment.

questions.text: There are more atoms in a single grain of sand than ways to shuffle a card deck.
questions.explanation: A 52-card deck has more possible orderings than that — about 8 followed by 67 zeros.

questions.text: If the Sun suddenly vanished, Earth would fly out of orbit instantly.
questions.explanation: Gravity's effect travels at light speed, so Earth would orbit normally for about 8 more minutes.

questions.text: Hydrogen and helium make up over 98 percent of all ordinary matter in the universe.
questions.explanation: These two lightest elements dominate; everything else is a tiny fraction of normal matter.

questions.text: James Cameron directed both Titanic and Avatar.
questions.explanation: He directed both, and each became the highest-grossing film of its era.

questions.text: Eminem's real first name is Marshall.
questions.explanation: His legal name is Marshall Bruce Mathers III.

questions.text: The Hollywood sign originally spelled out a longer word than 'Hollywood'.
questions.explanation: It read 'Hollywoodland', a real estate ad, until it was shortened.

questions.text: Netflix started out mailing DVDs to customers before it streamed anything.
questions.explanation: It ran a DVD-by-mail rental service years before launching streaming.

questions.text: The Simpsons has aired more episodes than any other US primetime scripted series.
questions.explanation: It surpassed every other primetime scripted show in episode count.

questions.text: India produces more feature films per year than the United States.
questions.explanation: India is consistently among the world's largest producers by film count.

questions.text: The clapperboard on a film set helps editors sync sound with picture.
questions.explanation: The sharp clap gives a matching audio spike and visual frame to align.

questions.text: The Beatles formed in London.
questions.explanation: They formed in Liverpool, not London.

questions.text: Pixar's first feature film was Finding Nemo.
questions.explanation: Toy Story (1995) was Pixar's first feature film.

questions.text: Leonardo DiCaprio won his first acting Oscar for Titanic.
questions.explanation: His first acting Oscar came for The Revenant in 2016.

questions.text: Audrey Hepburn sang all her own vocals in My Fair Lady.
questions.explanation: Her singing was dubbed by Marni Nixon.

questions.text: Tom Hanks has won more acting Oscars than any other performer.
questions.explanation: He won two; Katharine Hepburn won four, the record for acting.

questions.text: Spotify was founded in the United States.
questions.explanation: Spotify was founded in Stockholm, Sweden.

questions.text: Most commercial movie 'blood' in classic black-and-white films was bright red paint.
questions.explanation: In Psycho, Hitchcock used chocolate syrup, which read better on black-and-white film.

questions.text: A single recorded scream has been reused as a sound effect in hundreds of films.
questions.explanation: The 'Wilhelm Scream' is a stock effect dropped into films for decades.

questions.text: The same actor voiced Darth Vader and Mufasa in The Lion King.
questions.explanation: James Earl Jones voiced both characters.

questions.text: Sean Connery turned down the lead wizard role in The Lord of the Rings.
questions.explanation: He was offered Gandalf but declined, saying he didn't understand the script.

questions.text: In the original Wizard of Oz book, Dorothy's magic slippers were silver.
questions.explanation: They were changed to ruby red for the film to take advantage of Technicolor.

questions.text: Around 90 percent of Pixar's Toy Story 2 was once accidentally deleted during production.
questions.explanation: A stray delete command wiped the files; a home backup saved the film.

questions.text: The voices of Mickey Mouse and Minnie Mouse were once married in real life.
questions.explanation: Wayne Allwine and Russi Taylor, their voice actors, were married.

questions.text: Anthony Hopkins won a Best Actor Oscar with only about 16 minutes of screen time.
questions.explanation: His Silence of the Lambs role is among the shortest Oscar-winning leads.

questions.text: Charlie Chaplin reportedly once lost a Chaplin lookalike contest.
questions.explanation: He is widely said to have entered and failed to place first.

questions.text: The 'Happy Birthday' song earned royalties under copyright for decades.
questions.explanation: It generated licensing fees until a court ruled it public domain in 2016.

questions.text: A near-silent black-and-white film won Best Picture as recently as the 2010s.
questions.explanation: The Artist won the 2012 Best Picture Oscar.

questions.text: 'Hakuna Matata' from The Lion King is a real phrase in an African language.
questions.explanation: It's genuine Swahili, meaning roughly 'no worries'.

questions.text: A Mumbai cinema has screened the same Bollywood film daily since 1995.
questions.explanation: Dilwale Dulhania Le Jayenge has run at Maratha Mandir for decades.

questions.text: Walt Disney personally holds the record for most Oscars won by anyone.
questions.explanation: He won 22 competitive Academy Awards, more than any other individual.

questions.text: Walt Disney won more Oscars than any other person in a single year only.
questions.explanation: Disney holds the all-time record of 22 competitive Oscars, not just a single-year mark.

questions.text: Mickey Mouse was originally created and named by Walt Disney's brother Roy.
questions.explanation: Walt Disney and Ub Iwerks created Mickey; the early name considered was Mortimer.

questions.text: Justin Bieber's 'Baby' was the first video to reach a billion views online.
questions.explanation: Psy's Gangnam Style was the first to hit a billion YouTube views, in 2012.

questions.text: Prince could not play instruments and relied on session musicians for his records.
questions.explanation: Prince famously played dozens of instruments himself.

questions.text: The croissant was invented in France.
questions.explanation: Its crescent-shaped ancestor, the kipferl, originated in Austria.

questions.text: The actor who played Mr. Bean has no university education in any technical field.
questions.explanation: Rowan Atkinson holds a degree in electrical engineering.

questions.text: Stan Lee never appeared on screen in any of the Marvel movies.
questions.explanation: He made brief cameo appearances across dozens of Marvel films.

questions.text: The Wizard of Oz was the first feature film ever shot in color.
questions.explanation: Color features existed well before it; it's famous for Technicolor, not for being first.

questions.text: Lagaan won the Academy Award for Best Foreign Language Film.
questions.explanation: It was nominated but did not win the Oscar.

questions.text: Alfred Hitchcock won the Best Director Oscar multiple times in his career.
questions.explanation: Despite five nominations, he never won a competitive Best Director Oscar.

questions.text: Dadasaheb Phalke directed India's first sound film.
questions.explanation: His 1913 Raja Harishchandra, India's first feature, was silent.

questions.text: A.R. Rahman was the first Indian to be nominated for an Academy Award.
questions.explanation: Indians were nominated decades earlier; Rahman won two Oscars in 2009.

questions.text: Kaun Banega Crorepati was first hosted by Shah Rukh Khan.
questions.explanation: Amitabh Bachchan hosted the debut season.

questions.text: The first feature film with synchronized dialogue was The Jazz Singer.
questions.explanation: The 1927 film is credited as the first major 'talkie'.

questions.text: Satyajit Ray never received any recognition from the Academy Awards.
questions.explanation: He received an Academy Honorary Award in 1992.

questions.text: Rajinikanth worked as a bus conductor before becoming a film star.
questions.explanation: He worked as a bus conductor in Bangalore before entering cinema.

questions.text: The 1960 epic Mughal-e-Azam was filmed entirely in color.
questions.explanation: Only parts were in color; much of it was black and white.

questions.text: India's national anthem was written by a Nobel Prize winner.
questions.explanation: Rabindranath Tagore, a Nobel laureate, wrote 'Jana Gana Mana'.

questions.text: India's national anthem was written by Mahatma Gandhi.
questions.explanation: It was written by Rabindranath Tagore, a Nobel laureate.

questions.text: Disney's 'Frozen' was based on an entirely original story with no source material.
questions.explanation: It was loosely based on Hans Christian Andersen's 'The Snow Queen'.

questions.text: The movie 'Frozen' was based on a fairy tale by Hans Christian Andersen.
questions.explanation: It drew loosely from his story 'The Snow Queen'.

questions.text: Raj Kapoor's films were banned outright across the Soviet Union.
questions.explanation: They were hugely popular there; Awara became a Soviet cultural phenomenon.

questions.text: The Friends theme song was performed by a band called Maroon 5.
questions.explanation: 'I'll Be There for You' was recorded by The Rembrandts.

questions.text: Peanuts grow underground rather than on a tree.
questions.explanation: They form in pods beneath the soil, which is why they count as legumes.

questions.text: Honey can stay edible for thousands of years if sealed properly.
questions.explanation: Its low moisture and acidity block microbes, so it keeps almost indefinitely.

questions.text: Bananas grow curving upward toward the sky as they ripen.
questions.explanation: They bend up toward sunlight while growing on the plant.

questions.text: The Netherlands has more bicycles than it has people.
questions.explanation: Registered bikes outnumber the Dutch population.

questions.text: Tomato ketchup was sold in 1830s America as a medicine.
questions.explanation: It was marketed in pill form to treat stomach complaints.

questions.text: Sweden has more islands than any other country in the world.
questions.explanation: It has over 260,000 islands, the most of any nation, nearly all uninhabited.

questions.text: French fries were first created in France.
questions.explanation: The frying method is generally credited to Belgium, despite the name.

questions.text: The Caesar salad was named after Julius Caesar.
questions.explanation: It was named after Caesar Cardini, who created it in Tijuana, Mexico in 1924.

questions.text: Champagne can come from any wine region in France.
questions.explanation: Only sparkling wine from the Champagne region may legally be called champagne.

questions.text: The capital city of Australia is Sydney.
questions.explanation: The capital is Canberra, chosen as a compromise between Sydney and Melbourne.

questions.text: Carrots were orange long before any other color was grown.
questions.explanation: Early carrots were mostly purple and yellow; orange came later.

questions.text: The croissant was invented in France.
questions.explanation: Its crescent-shaped ancestor, the kipferl, came from Austria.

questions.text: Most vanilla flavoring is made from real vanilla beans.
questions.explanation: The large majority is synthetic vanillin; real beans supply only a small share.

questions.text: Cashews grow hanging beneath a soft, edible fruit.
questions.explanation: Each nut dangles under a cashew apple, a fleshy fruit eaten in many regions.

questions.text: Most wasabi served with sushi outside Japan is dyed horseradish.
questions.explanation: Real wasabi is costly and perishable, so substitutes are standard abroad.

questions.text: Bell peppers and hot chili peppers are the same plant species.
questions.explanation: Both are Capsicum cultivars; bell peppers just lack the heat compound.

questions.text: Chicken tikka masala is widely thought to have been invented in Britain.
questions.explanation: Its commonly cited origin is a British curry house, not South Asia.

questions.text: Almonds come from a fruit closely related to the peach.
questions.explanation: An almond is the seed of a stone fruit in the same family as peaches.

questions.text: Eating a lot of nutmeg can cause toxic, hallucinogenic effects.
questions.explanation: It contains myristicin, which is toxic and unpleasant in high doses.

questions.text: Pineapples grow on tall palm-like trees.
questions.explanation: They grow low to the ground on a leafy plant, one fruit per plant.

questions.text: A strawberry is botanically a true berry.
questions.explanation: It isn't; its seeds sit on the outside, which true berries don't do.

questions.text: Saffron is worth more than its weight in gold.
questions.explanation: It's the most expensive spice but still far cheaper than gold by weight.

questions.text: Tea was first cultivated in India before spreading worldwide.
questions.explanation: Tea cultivation began in China; India's industry came much later.

questions.text: A pineapple plant grows a new fruit every few weeks once mature.
questions.explanation: It takes roughly two years to produce a single pineapple.

questions.text: Mount Everest is the tallest mountain on Earth from base to summit.
questions.explanation: Hawaii's Mauna Kea is taller base-to-peak; Everest is highest above sea level.

questions.text: Water makes up the majority of a fresh cucumber's weight.
questions.explanation: A cucumber is roughly 95 percent water.

questions.text: Antarctica is classed as a desert.
questions.explanation: It gets very little precipitation, which is the definition of a desert.

questions.text: Bubble wrap was originally invented to be used as wallpaper.
questions.explanation: Its inventors first marketed it as textured wallpaper before packaging.

questions.text: Ketchup originated as a fermented fish sauce in Asia.
questions.explanation: Early 'ke-tsiap' was a fish-based sauce long before the tomato version.

questions.text: The Eiffel Tower was built as a temporary structure.
questions.explanation: It had a 20-year permit and was nearly torn down before radio use saved it.

questions.text: Lobster was once considered a low-class food fed to prisoners.
questions.explanation: In colonial America it was cheap, abundant, and seen as poor people's food.

questions.text: Goulash originated in Germany.
questions.explanation: Goulash is a Hungarian dish, dating back to medieval Magyar herdsmen.

questions.text: The fortune cookie is a traditional Chinese invention.
questions.explanation: It was popularized in the United States and is rare in China itself.

questions.text: Vodka was originally invented in France.
questions.explanation: Vodka's origins lie in Eastern Europe, in Russia and Poland.

questions.text: The Great Wall of China is a single continuous wall.
questions.explanation: It's a network of many walls and sections built over different dynasties.

questions.text: Dutch farmers bred orange carrots to honor their royal House of Orange.
questions.explanation: That's a popular myth; orange carrots predate and weren't bred to honor them.

questions.text: The dish chicken tikka masala is an ancient traditional Indian recipe.
questions.explanation: It's widely believed to have been created in Britain, not ancient India.

questions.text: Cucumbers and watermelons belong to the same plant family.
questions.explanation: Both are part of the gourd family, Cucurbitaceae.

questions.text: Some cheeses are aged inside caves to develop their flavor.
questions.explanation: Cave aging gives cheeses like Roquefort their distinctive character.

questions.text: There is a country that has two separate capital cities for different functions.
questions.explanation: Several do; South Africa famously has three capitals for different branches.

questions.text: Pineapple contains an enzyme that can break down protein, including in your mouth.
questions.explanation: Bromelain digests protein, which is why pineapple can sting the tongue.

questions.text: Vanilla is the second most expensive spice in the world after saffron.
questions.explanation: Its labor-intensive hand pollination makes it extremely costly.

questions.text: There is a country where it's possible to be in two continents within one city.
questions.explanation: Istanbul straddles Europe and Asia across the Bosphorus.

questions.text: Apples belong to the same plant family as roses.
questions.explanation: Apples are in the Rosaceae family along with roses, pears, and cherries.

questions.text: Brussels sprouts get their name from a city in Germany.
questions.explanation: They're named after Brussels, the capital of Belgium, not a German city.

questions.text: Most table salt eaten worldwide is mined from ancient underground deposits only.
questions.explanation: A large share comes from evaporated seawater, not only mined rock salt.

questions.text: The chili pepper's heat is concentrated mainly in its seeds.
questions.explanation: The heat comes mostly from the white pith, not the seeds themselves.

questions.text: Wild bananas naturally contain no seeds at all.
questions.explanation: Wild bananas are full of large hard seeds; seedless ones are cultivated.

questions.text: Drinking alcohol warms up your body's core temperature.
questions.explanation: It makes you feel warm but actually lowers core body temperature.

questions.text: The world's hottest chili peppers can be hot enough to be medically dangerous.
questions.explanation: Extreme superhot peppers have sent people to hospital with severe reactions.

questions.text: The FIFA World Cup is contested by national teams rather than club sides.
questions.explanation: The men's FIFA World Cup is played by national teams representing their countries.

questions.text: The first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay.
questions.explanation: Uruguay hosted the first FIFA World Cup in 1930 and also won it.

questions.text: The World Cup final is played over two legs.
questions.explanation: The World Cup final is a single match.

questions.text: A World Cup group-stage match can end in a draw.
questions.explanation: Group-stage matches can finish level, with each team receiving one point.

questions.text: A team must win every match to win the World Cup.
questions.explanation: Teams can draw or even lose earlier matches and still win the tournament.

questions.text: The World Cup has always been played every four years without interruption.
questions.explanation: The 1942 and 1946 tournaments were cancelled because of World War II.

questions.text: Brazil has won more men's World Cups than any other country.
questions.explanation: Brazil have won the men's FIFA World Cup five times.

questions.text: Only European teams have ever won the men's FIFA World Cup.
questions.explanation: South American teams such as Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay have won it.

questions.text: The host nation automatically qualifies for the World Cup.
questions.explanation: The host nation receives automatic qualification for the tournament.

questions.text: Argentina won the 2022 FIFA World Cup.
questions.explanation: Argentina beat France in the 2022 final in Qatar.

questions.text: The 2022 World Cup was played during the usual June-July window.
questions.explanation: Qatar 2022 was played in November and December.

questions.text: Penalty shoot-outs can decide World Cup knockout matches.
questions.explanation: Knockout matches tied after extra time can be decided by penalties.

questions.text: The winner keeps the original World Cup trophy forever.
questions.explanation: Winners receive the trophy temporarily and are given a replica to keep.

questions.text: South Africa hosted the first World Cup on African soil.
questions.explanation: South Africa 2010 was the first men's FIFA World Cup hosted in Africa.

questions.text: Qatar hosted the first World Cup held in the Middle East.
questions.explanation: Qatar 2022 was the first men's FIFA World Cup held in the Middle East.

questions.text: The 2026 World Cup will have 48 teams.
questions.explanation: The 2026 men's World Cup expands from 32 to 48 teams.

questions.text: The 2026 World Cup will be hosted by two countries.
questions.explanation: It will be hosted by Canada, Mexico, and the United States.

questions.text: Brazil has missed at least one men's World Cup.
questions.explanation: Brazil have appeared in every men's FIFA World Cup through 2022.

questions.text: Morocco was the first African team to reach a men's World Cup semi-final.
questions.explanation: Morocco reached the semi-finals at Qatar 2022.

questions.text: A red card means the player can be replaced immediately.
questions.explanation: A sent-off player cannot be replaced during the match.

questions.text: The World Cup includes a group stage before knockouts.
questions.explanation: Modern World Cups use a group phase followed by knockout rounds.

questions.text: England has won the men's World Cup more than once.
questions.explanation: England's only men's World Cup title came in 1966.

questions.text: Pele won three men's World Cups as a player.
questions.explanation: Pele was part of Brazil's winning squads in 1958, 1962, and 1970.

questions.text: The first World Cup final was played between Uruguay and Argentina.
questions.explanation: Uruguay beat Argentina 4-2 in the 1930 final.

questions.text: The 1950 World Cup did not have a traditional one-match final.
questions.explanation: The champion was decided through a final round-robin group.

questions.text: The first World Cup penalty shoot-out happened before the 1980s.
questions.explanation: The first World Cup penalty shoot-out was West Germany vs France in 1982.

questions.text: The 1998 World Cup was the first men's edition with 32 teams.
questions.explanation: France 1998 expanded the tournament to 32 teams.

questions.text: The 1970 World Cup introduced yellow and red cards.
questions.explanation: The yellow and red card system was introduced at Mexico 1970.

questions.text: The stolen Jules Rimet Trophy was recovered by police during a raid in 1966.
questions.explanation: It was found wrapped in newspaper under a hedge by a dog named Pickles.

questions.text: The original Jules Rimet Trophy is still displayed at FIFA headquarters.
questions.explanation: It was stolen in Brazil in 1983 and has never been recovered.

questions.text: The Jules Rimet Trophy was stolen twice.
questions.explanation: It was stolen in England in 1966 and again in Brazil in 1983.

questions.text: The first World Cup match ball was specially designed to show up on color television.
questions.explanation: The black-and-white Telstar was designed for visibility on black-and-white TV.

questions.text: The 1970 World Cup used the first adidas World Cup match ball.
questions.explanation: The adidas Telstar debuted at Mexico 1970.

questions.text: Miroslav Klose is the top scorer in men's World Cup history.
questions.explanation: Klose scored 16 World Cup goals for Germany.

questions.text: Just Fontaine's record 13 goals were spread across two World Cup tournaments.
questions.explanation: He scored all 13 in a single tournament, the 1958 World Cup.

questions.text: Pele scored in a World Cup final before turning 18.
questions.explanation: Pele scored in the 1958 final as a 17-year-old.

questions.text: The most goals one player has scored in a single World Cup match is four.
questions.explanation: Oleg Salenko scored five for Russia against Cameroon in 1994.

questions.text: No player over the age of 40 has ever scored at a men's World Cup.
questions.explanation: Roger Milla scored for Cameroon at age 42 in 1994.

questions.text: Cristiano Ronaldo holds the record for most men's World Cup appearances.
questions.explanation: Lionel Messi holds that record, reaching 26 World Cup appearances.

questions.text: The World Cup Golden Boot always goes to a player from the winning team.
questions.explanation: The top scorer often comes from another team.

questions.text: France won their first men's World Cup before Brazil won their first.
questions.explanation: Brazil first won in 1958; France first won in 1998.

questions.text: A World Cup final has ended goalless after extra time.
questions.explanation: Brazil vs Italy in 1994 finished 0-0 after extra time.

questions.text: Germany has played in a men's World Cup final decided by penalties.
questions.explanation: Germany's men's World Cup finals have not been decided by penalty shoot-outs.

questions.text: The 1930 World Cup had no qualification tournament.
questions.explanation: The first World Cup was invitation-based rather than qualification-based.

questions.text: All 1930 World Cup matches were played in one city.
questions.explanation: All matches at the 1930 World Cup were played in Montevideo.

questions.text: The highest-scoring World Cup match finished with nine total goals.
questions.explanation: Austria beat Switzerland 7-5 in 1954, for twelve total goals.

questions.text: The fastest goal in World Cup history came after about two minutes.
questions.explanation: Hakan Sukur scored after roughly 11 seconds in 2002.

questions.text: Only one player has ever scored a hat-trick in a World Cup final.
questions.explanation: Geoff Hurst did it in 1966 and Kylian Mbappe did it in 2022.

questions.text: Spain won the 2010 World Cup after losing their opening match.
questions.explanation: Spain lost to Switzerland in their opener and still won the tournament.

questions.text: Italy once won the World Cup as host nation.
questions.explanation: Italy hosted and won the 1934 World Cup.

questions.text: No goalkeeper has ever been sent off in a men's World Cup match.
questions.explanation: Italy's Gianluca Pagliuca was sent off at the 1994 World Cup.

questions.text: Carlos Caszely received the first physical red card in World Cup history.
questions.explanation: Caszely was sent off for Chile against West Germany in 1974.

questions.text: The 1994 World Cup final was played indoors.
questions.explanation: It was played outdoors at the Rose Bowl in Pasadena.

questions.text: India has played at the men's FIFA World Cup.
questions.explanation: India qualified by default for 1950 but withdrew and has never played in the tournament.

questions.text: No team has ever won a World Cup hosted on another continent.
questions.explanation: Brazil, a South American side, won the 1958 World Cup in Sweden.

questions.text: Uruguay needed only a draw against Brazil in 1950 to become champions.
questions.explanation: Uruguay had to beat Brazil in the decisive 1950 match, which they did.

questions.text: Only one host nation has ever won the World Cup on home soil.
questions.explanation: Several have, including Uruguay, Italy, England, Argentina, and France.

questions.text: Every men's World Cup host has reached at least the semi-finals.
questions.explanation: Several hosts have failed to reach the semi-finals.

questions.text: Mexico has hosted the men's FIFA World Cup more than once.
questions.explanation: Mexico hosted the tournament in 1970 and 1986, and is a co-host again in 2026.

questions.text: Scotland reached the knockout stage of the 1974 World Cup unbeaten.
questions.explanation: They were unbeaten but were eliminated in the group stage on goal difference.

questions.text: The 1974 World Cup was the first to use the current trophy design.
questions.explanation: The current FIFA World Cup Trophy debuted in 1974 after Brazil kept the Jules Rimet Trophy.

questions.text: A team once won the World Cup after scoring only eight goals in the tournament.
questions.explanation: Spain won the 2010 World Cup while scoring only eight goals in seven matches.

questions.text: Spain won every knockout match at the 2010 World Cup by the same scoreline.
questions.explanation: Spain won all four knockout matches in 2010 by 1-0.

questions.text: The first World Cup final used the same ball for both halves.
questions.explanation: The 1930 final used Argentina's ball in the first half and Uruguay's ball in the second.

questions.text: A defending champion has been eliminated from the next World Cup group stage.
questions.explanation: Defending champions including France (2002), Spain (2014), and Germany (2018) went out in the group stage.

questions.text: The record crowd for a single World Cup match was set back in 1950.
questions.explanation: About 173,850 packed Rio's Maracanã for the 1950 deciding match, still a record.

questions.text: The most recent World Cup drew the highest total attendance in the tournament's history.
questions.explanation: The 1994 USA World Cup still holds the record for total tournament attendance.

questions.text: A European nation has won the World Cup on South American soil.
questions.explanation: No European team has ever won a World Cup held in the Americas.

questions.text: Goalkeepers are allowed to be named captain of a World Cup-winning team.
questions.explanation: Captains like Dino Zoff led their sides to World Cup glory from in goal.

questions.text: Pelé scored in two different World Cup finals.
questions.explanation: He scored in the 1958 final as a teenager and again in the 1970 final.

questions.text: The same stadium has hosted two different World Cup finals.
questions.explanation: Mexico City's Estadio Azteca hosted the 1970 and 1986 finals.

questions.text: No person has ever won the World Cup both as a player and as a head coach.
questions.explanation: Mário Zagallo and Franz Beckenbauer each achieved both.

questions.text: No goalkeeper has ever captained a World Cup-winning side.
questions.explanation: Italy's Dino Zoff captained the 1982 champions from goal.

questions.text: A goalkeeper has saved multiple penalties in a single World Cup shoot-out.
questions.explanation: Keepers like Ricardo for Portugal in 2006 saved several spot-kicks in one shoot-out.

questions.text: The Jules Rimet Trophy was permanently awarded to the first team to win three titles.
questions.explanation: Brazil kept it permanently after their third title in 1970.

questions.text: A World Cup has been hosted by two countries jointly.
questions.explanation: South Korea and Japan co-hosted the 2002 tournament.

questions.text: The World Cup was once hosted by a country that had never previously qualified for it.
questions.explanation: Qatar hosted in 2022 having never qualified for a World Cup before.

questions.text: A team has won the World Cup without conceding a single goal in the knockout rounds.
questions.explanation: Spain conceded none in the 2010 knockout stage, winning each match 1-0.

questions.text: No player has scored in four different World Cup tournaments.
questions.explanation: Players including Pelé and Uwe Seeler found the net across four different World Cups.

questions.text: Cheetahs are the fastest land animals in the world.
questions.explanation: They can reach speeds up to 60-70 mph.

questions.text: A group of crows is called a 'murder'.
questions.explanation: Yes, it is true! It comes from old folk tales.

questions.text: Bats are completely blind.
questions.explanation: Bats can actually see quite well, some even better than humans in low light.

questions.text: Sloths can hold their breath longer than dolphins.
questions.explanation: By slowing their heart rate, sloths can hold their breath for up to 40 minutes!

questions.text: Bullfrogs do not sleep.
questions.explanation: They enter periods of resting but do not experience deep sleep.

questions.text: Koalas fingerprints are so close to humans that they can taint crime scenes.
questions.explanation: Even under a microscope, their fingerprints are nearly indistinguishable from humans.

questions.text: Goldfish have a three-second memory.
questions.explanation: Goldfish actually have a memory span of several months.

questions.text: Wombat poop is cube-shaped.
questions.explanation: This shape stops the poop from rolling away and marks their territory.

questions.text: An octopus has four hearts.
questions.explanation: An octopus actually has exactly three hearts.

questions.text: The Turritopsis dohrnii jellyfish is biologically immortal.
questions.explanation: It can revert completely to a sexually immature stage after having reached sexual maturity.

questions.text: Pigs cannot look up into the sky.
questions.explanation: The anatomy of their neck prevents them from looking straight up.

questions.text: Ostriches bury their heads in the sand when scared.
questions.explanation: They lie low to hide or turn eggs in their nests, but do not bury their heads.

questions.text: A snail can sleep for three years.
questions.explanation: They can hibernate for years if the weather is not suitable for survival.

questions.text: Dolphins can only sleep fully submerged underwater.
questions.explanation: Dolphins sleep at the surface with one eye open, keeping half their brain awake.

questions.text: Bulls are enraged by the color red.
questions.explanation: Bulls are colorblind; the movement of the cape angers them, not the color.

questions.text: Elephants can jump about 6 inches off the ground.
questions.explanation: Elephants cannot jump at all due to their bone structure and weight.

questions.text: A cockroach can live for a week without its head.
questions.explanation: They breathe through body spiracles and die only from thirst.

questions.text: Polar bears have white skin beneath their fur.
questions.explanation: Their skin is black to absorb sunlight; only the fur appears white.

questions.text: Owls can rotate their heads a full 360 degrees.
questions.explanation: They can only turn their heads 270 degrees, not a full circle.

questions.text: A flea can jump 350 times its own body length.
questions.explanation: That is equivalent to a human jumping the length of a football field.

questions.text: Honeybees have four eyes.
questions.explanation: Honeybees actually have five eyes — two compound and three simple ocelli.

questions.text: Most penguins mate for life.
questions.explanation: Many species only stay together for one breeding season.

questions.text: Hummingbirds are the only birds that can fly backwards.
questions.explanation: Their unique shoulder joint allows for full rotational flight.

questions.text: Crocodiles shed tears when eating.
questions.explanation: Swallowing pumps air into sinuses near their eyes, causing a frothing effect.

questions.text: Mice love cheese above all other foods.
questions.explanation: Mice actually prefer sweet and grain-based foods over cheese.

questions.text: A group of flamingos is called a flamboyance.
questions.explanation: Perhaps the most fitting collective noun in the animal kingdom.

questions.text: Starfish have no brain.
questions.explanation: They have a decentralized nervous system spread across their entire body.

questions.text: Sea otters drift apart during sleep because they lack social bonds.
questions.explanation: Sea otters actually hold hands while sleeping to stay together as a raft.

questions.text: A shrimp heart is located in its head.
questions.explanation: All of a shrimp vital organs are packed into the cephalothorax.

questions.text: Flamingos are born pink because of genetics.
questions.explanation: Flamingos are actually born white; their pink color comes from carotenoids in their diet.

questions.text: A group of owls is called a parliament.
questions.explanation: Owls have long been associated with wisdom and governance.

questions.text: Sharks must keep swimming or they will die.
questions.explanation: Most sharks can rest on the ocean floor; only some obligate ram ventilators must keep moving.

questions.text: Butterflies taste with their feet.
questions.explanation: They have chemoreceptors on their legs to identify plants for egg-laying.

questions.text: A group of cats is called a clowder.
questions.explanation: Lesser known collective nouns are common in English for animal groups.

questions.text: Wombats produce square-shaped droppings.
questions.explanation: Their intestines are shaped to produce cube-like scat to mark territory.

questions.text: Lobsters are immortal.
questions.explanation: They do not weaken with age but still die from disease, predation, or molting.

questions.text: Cows are solitary creatures that prefer being alone.
questions.explanation: Research shows cows form close bonds with best friends and become stressed when separated.

questions.text: A blue whale heart is so large a child could swim through its arteries.
questions.explanation: The heart weighs around 400 pounds and is the size of a small car.

questions.text: Snakes can hear through their jawbone.
questions.explanation: They detect vibrations through ground contact with their jaw.

questions.text: Honeybees die after stinging because their stinger stays in mammals.
questions.explanation: The barbed stinger gets lodged and tears away part of the bee abdomen.

questions.text: A group of jellyfish is called a bloom or a smack.
questions.explanation: Both terms are accepted collective nouns for jellyfish.

questions.text: Gorillas cannot swim.
questions.explanation: They lack the instinctive paddle reflex and generally avoid deep water.

questions.text: Horses can sleep standing up but can only dream lying down.
questions.explanation: They need REM sleep which requires lying down.

questions.text: Octopuses have one heart, like most sea creatures.
questions.explanation: Octopuses actually have three hearts and copper-based blue blood.

questions.text: Pigeons have been awarded military medals.
questions.explanation: Carrier pigeons like Cher Ami were decorated for service in World War I.

questions.text: Cats always land on their feet.
questions.explanation: They have a righting reflex but still sustain injuries from falls, especially short ones.

questions.text: Reindeer eyes change color between summer and winter.
questions.explanation: Their eyes shift from gold in summer to blue in winter to improve vision in low arctic light.

questions.text: A mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a bullet.
questions.explanation: It strikes at 50 mph and can shatter aquarium glass.

questions.text: Clownfish can change sex.
questions.explanation: When the dominant female dies, the dominant male transitions to female.

questions.text: Dogs sweat through their paws.
questions.explanation: Merocrine sweat glands are located in their paw pads.

questions.text: The Titanic sank on its maiden voyage.
questions.explanation: It sank in 1912 while traveling from Southampton to New York.

questions.text: George Washington had wooden teeth.
questions.explanation: His dentures were made from ivory, lead, brass, and other humans' teeth.

questions.text: Julius Caesar was the first Emperor of Rome.
questions.explanation: Augustus was the first Roman Emperor.

questions.text: The shortest war in history lasted only 38 minutes.
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896.

questions.text: Abraham Lincoln is in the Wrestling Hall of Fame.
questions.explanation: He was an outstanding wrestler in his youth, losing only one out of 300 matches.

questions.text: Paul Revere shouted 'The British are coming!'
questions.explanation: The mission relied on secrecy, so he quietly warned local leaders.

questions.text: The ancient Romans used urine as mouthwash.
questions.explanation: Ammonia in urine was considered a bleaching agent.

questions.text: Witches were burned at the stake during the Salem Witch Trials.
questions.explanation: Most were hanged, and one was pressed to death. None were burned.

questions.text: There was a famous Pope who dug up his predecessor's corpse to put it on trial.
questions.explanation: The 'Cadaver Synod' of 897, where Pope Formosus was posthumously tried.

questions.text: Albert Einstein was offered the presidency of Israel.
questions.explanation: He was offered the role in 1952 but politely declined.

questions.text: Cleopatra was ethnically Egyptian.
questions.explanation: She was of Greek Macedonian descent from the Ptolemaic dynasty.

questions.text: The Hundred Years War lasted exactly 100 years.
questions.explanation: It lasted 116 years, from 1337 to 1453.

questions.text: The Egyptian pyramids were built by slaves.
questions.explanation: Archaeological evidence shows they were built by organized paid workers.

questions.text: Abraham Lincoln held a US patent.
questions.explanation: He patented a device to lift boats over shallow water, the only president to do so.

questions.text: Alexander the Great never lost a battle.
questions.explanation: He fought for 15 years across three continents without a single military defeat.

questions.text: Roman gladiators rarely fought to the death.
questions.explanation: They were expensive to train; promoters preferred surrenders over fatalities.

questions.text: Oxford University existed before the Aztec Empire.
questions.explanation: Oxford dates to around 1096; Tenochtitlan was founded in 1325.

questions.text: Marie Antoinette said Let them eat cake.
questions.explanation: No credible historical evidence exists that she ever said this.

questions.text: Winston Churchill was born in a ladies cloakroom.
questions.explanation: His mother went into premature labor at a ball at Blenheim Palace.

questions.text: More people lived in the Americas than in Europe before Columbus arrived.
questions.explanation: Estimates place the pre-Columbian Americas population at 60 to 100 million.

questions.text: Magellan was the first person to sail around the entire world.
questions.explanation: He died in the Philippines; his crew under Elcano actually completed the voyage.

questions.text: Thomas Edison personally invented the light bulb.
questions.explanation: He improved and commercialized it; Joseph Swan had earlier working prototypes.

questions.text: In Ancient Egypt, killing a cat was punishable by death.
questions.explanation: Cats were considered sacred, and harming one was a serious crime.

questions.text: The shortest recorded war in history lasted only 38 minutes.
questions.explanation: The Anglo-Zanzibar War of 1896 lasted between 38 and 45 minutes.

questions.text: Joan of Arc was burned at the stake for witchcraft.
questions.explanation: She was convicted of heresy and gender nonconformity for wearing men clothing.

questions.text: Ancient Rome had a goddess dedicated to the city sewer system.
questions.explanation: Cloacina was the deity protecting the Cloaca Maxima, Rome main sewer.

questions.text: Napoleon Bonaparte was unusually short for his era.
questions.explanation: He was around 5 foot 7, average for a French man of his time.

questions.text: Albert Einstein failed maths at school.
questions.explanation: He excelled at maths and physics; the myth comes from a misread of Swiss report cards.

questions.text: Albert Einstein was offered the presidency of Israel and declined.
questions.explanation: He was offered the role in 1952 and politely turned it down.

questions.text: The Viking helmet with horns is a historical myth.
questions.explanation: No horned Viking helmets have ever been found by archaeologists; it was invented by artists.

questions.text: Ancient Greeks and Romans had a concept of human rights.
questions.explanation: Modern human rights are a post-Enlightenment concept; ancient societies had no equivalent.

questions.text: The first photograph ever taken required an eight-hour exposure.
questions.explanation: Joseph Nicephore Niepce took the earliest surviving photo around 1826-27.

questions.text: The Great Fire of London destroyed St Paul Cathedral.
questions.explanation: The old Gothic cathedral burned down; Wren designed its famous replacement.

questions.text: Julius Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March, or March 15th.
questions.explanation: He was stabbed 23 times by a group of senators in 44 BC.

questions.text: The Berlin Wall fell in 1991.
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on 9 November 1989; Germany reunified in October 1990.

questions.text: Ancient Egyptians shaved their eyebrows when a pet cat died.
questions.explanation: It was a sign of mourning; they also shaved eyebrows when dogs died.

questions.text: The Colosseum in Rome could hold up to 80,000 spectators.
questions.explanation: Modern estimates put its capacity between 50,000 and 80,000 people.

questions.text: King Henry VIII had six wives and executed all of them.
questions.explanation: He had six wives but only executed two; others were divorced or died naturally.

questions.text: Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize multiple times but never won.
questions.explanation: He was nominated five times; the Nobel Committee later expressed regret at not awarding him.

questions.text: The Titanic sank on her maiden voyage in 1912.
questions.explanation: She sank on 15 April 1912 after hitting an iceberg.

questions.text: Genghis Khan killed so many people that global temperatures dropped.
questions.explanation: His campaigns depopulated vast areas, allowing forests to regrow and absorb enough CO2 to affect climate.

questions.text: The first country to give women the right to vote was the United States.
questions.explanation: New Zealand was the first in 1893; the US followed in 1920.

questions.text: The Eiffel Tower was originally intended to be demolished after 20 years.
questions.explanation: It was built as a temporary structure for the 1889 World Fair.

questions.text: Michelangelo was still working as an architect in his 80s.
questions.explanation: He worked on St Peter Basilica in Rome until close to his death at age 88.

questions.text: The Roman Empire and the Han Dynasty of China coexisted at the same time.
questions.explanation: Both were superpowers from roughly 200 BC to 200 AD and even knew of each other.

questions.text: Ancient Chinese emperors were buried with armies of terracotta soldiers.
questions.explanation: China First Emperor Qin Shi Huang had over 8,000 life-size soldiers buried with him.

questions.text: The Aztec capital Tenochtitlan was larger than most European cities at the time of conquest.
questions.explanation: With 200,000 to 300,000 people, it dwarfed contemporary London or Paris.

questions.text: Christopher Columbus discovered America.
questions.explanation: Indigenous peoples lived there for millennia; Norse explorer Leif Erikson also preceded Columbus.

questions.text: Isaac Newton discovered gravity when an apple fell on his head.
questions.explanation: The apple story is largely mythologized; he observed falling apples but was not struck by one.

questions.text: Socrates never wrote any books or texts himself.
questions.explanation: All we know of Socrates comes through the writings of his students like Plato.
